title: Bypass zero-wait load-use bubbles
motivation: CoreMark spends a lot of time in tight loops with a load feeding the very next ALU, branch, or store. The current one-cycle load-use bubble leaves that zero-wait case idle even though the value is already on the MEM side in time to forward.

hypothesis.motivation: CoreMark spends a lot of time in tight loops with a load feeding the very next ALU, branch, or store. The current one-cycle load-use bubble leaves that zero-wait case idle even though the value is already on the MEM side in time to forward.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a MEM-stage load-result bypass so the instruction immediately behind a load can consume the value without stalling when `io_dmemReady=1`. Keep the existing freeze path for true bus backpressure, but stop treating the ordinary load-use pattern as an unconditional bubble.

title: Static backward-taken fetch predictor
motivation:
CoreMark spends a lot of time in tight loops, and the current core
always fetches sequentially until EX resolves a branch. That leaves a
full taken-branch penalty on the hot path even when the branch is
obviously backward.

hypothesis.motivation:

always fetches sequentially until EX resolves a branch. That leaves a
full taken-branch penalty on the hot path even when the branch is
obviously backward.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny IF-stage static predictor that speculatively redirects the
PC for backward conditional branches and all JALs, while leaving EX
redirect as the recovery path for mispredicts. This should trim the
average control-flow penalty on CoreMark loops with little LUT or Fmax
cost.

title: Load-to-store bypass
motivation:
CoreMark still mixes loads and stores in tight inner loops, and the
current one-size-fits-all load-use bubble wastes a cycle even when the
dependent consumer is only a store-data path that can be satisfied
later. Relaxing just that safe subset should recover IPC without
changing the branch predictor or the RVFI contract.

existing backpressure behavior when the bus is not ready.

title: Safe zero-wait load bypass
motivation:
CoreMark spends a lot of time in load-fed inner loops, and the current
one-cycle load-use bubble leaves the EX stage idle even when a zero-wait
memory response can already supply the value in time. Recovering that
cycle on the common ready path should improve IPC without changing the
RVFI contract or the branch predictor behavior.

memory response can already supply the value in time. Recovering that
cycle on the common ready path should improve IPC without changing the
RVFI contract or the branch predictor behavior.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a direct MEM-stage load-result bypass into the EX operand path so the
instruction immediately behind a load can consume the live data on
zero-wait cycles. Keep the existing interlock whenever memory is not
ready, so the core still stalls safely under bus backpressure instead of
letting a dependent consumer outrun the load.

title: Split slow divider off hot path
motivation:
CoreMark is mostly branch, load, and simple ALU traffic. The shared
combinational DIV/REM logic is a bad fit for that hot path because it
stretches the execute cone and burns LUTs even when division is rare.
Isolating the slow quotient/remainder work should raise Fmax without
disturbing the common single-cycle ops.

pipeline when one of those instructions executes. That removes the divider
from the common execute timing arc while preserving RV32M correctness and
letting CoreMark pay the latency only on the rare divide path.

title: Load-to-store replay
motivation:
CoreMark still has hot load/store chains in inner loops, and the current
load-use interlock treats store data like an EX-stage consumer. That is
conservative: the store payload is only needed at MEM, where the just-loaded
value can already be replayed from the current WB result. Removing that
unnecessary bubble should recover IPC without changing the RVFI contract.

unnecessary bubble should recover IPC without changing the RVFI contract.
hypothesis.hypothesis:

dependencies, but exempt the immediate load->store-data case. In MEM, replay
the current WB writeback value onto `dmem_wdata`/`mem_wdata` when a store's
`rs2` matches the load destination, while leaving `rs2_val` and the RVFI
source snapshot untouched. That should eliminate a safe bubble in
load/store-heavy CoreMark loops without widening the control-flow path.

motivation:

current ALU still drags DIV/REM combinational logic through the execute cone.

one-cycle datapath.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

motivation:

still treats a load followed immediately by a store as a full load-use
hazard even though the store only needs the loaded value by MEM, not by EX.
Recovering that bubble should improve IPC without touching the branch
predictor or the RVFI retirement contract.

title: Predecode fetch entries
motivation:
CoreMark is still front-end sensitive, and the current queue only stores
raw `pc`/`instr`/`next_pc`. ID still redoes full decode and immediate
generation on the hot consumption path, so the IF/ID boundary keeps more
logic than it needs and the control bundle is rebuilt every cycle.

when an instruction enters the queue, then carry that bundle forward so

branch policy and EX redirect recovery unchanged; moving decode and imm
assembly off the hot ID path should shorten the front-end critical path

title: Zero-wait load-use bypass
motivation:
CoreMark still spends a lot of time in tight load-fed loops. The current
one-cycle load-use bubble leaves the consumer idle even on zero-wait
memory cycles where the loaded value could be forwarded immediately.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark still spends a lot of time in tight load-fed loops. The current
one-cycle load-use bubble leaves the consumer idle even on zero-wait
memory cycles where the loaded value could be forwarded immediately.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a direct ready-load bypass from MEM into the EX operand path so the
instruction behind a load can consume the loaded value without stalling
when the data bus is not backpressured. Keep the existing interlock for
true bus stalls so correctness and RVFI-visible retirement stay intact.

title: Split fetch metadata lane
motivation:
CoreMark is still front-end sensitive, but the earlier predecode attempt
showed that widening the fetch queue can push the critical path upstream.
Keeping the instruction payload separate from the small next-PC metadata
should preserve the registered fetch-stream win without reintroducing that
width penalty.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark is still front-end sensitive, but the earlier predecode attempt
showed that widening the fetch queue can push the critical path upstream.
Keeping the instruction payload separate from the small next-PC metadata
should preserve the registered fetch-stream win without reintroducing that
width penalty.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the 2-entry fetch buffer lean, with the main payload carrying only
pc/instr/valid while a separate narrow sidecar tracks the predicted next PC
for each entry. That preserves the existing static backward/JAL policy and
EX redirect recovery, but avoids embedding wider control metadata in the
queue entry itself. The goal is to shorten the IF timing cone without
changing retirement order or RVFI-visible behavior.

changes.description: Split fetch payload from prediction metadata so the queue stays narrow while still carrying the predicted next PC for each buffered instruction.
Add short-stall and redirect regressions to verify the split fetch metadata path keeps RVFI order and flush behavior intact.

title: Commit-safe return stack
motivation:
CoreMark still spends time in helper-call and return-heavy paths. The
existing static backward/JAL predictor already handles the easy loop and
direct-call cases, so JALR returns are the remaining hot control-flow
misses worth attacking.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark still spends time in helper-call and return-heavy paths. The
existing static backward/JAL predictor already handles the easy loop and
direct-call cases, so JALR returns are the remaining hot control-flow
misses worth attacking.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny IF-stage return-address stack that predicts only exact
`jalr x0, x1/x5, 0` returns when the top entry matches. Train it only
from architectural retirements: push return addresses for retired
JAL/JALR calls that write x1/x5, pop on retired returns, and saturate on
overflow instead of overwriting older entries. Keep the current static
backward/JAL heuristic as fallback and let EX redirect recover misses.

title: Stall-safe ready-load bypass
motivation:
CoreMark spends a lot of time in tight load-fed loops. The current one-cycle
load-use bubble leaves the consumer idle even on zero-wait memory cycles
where the loaded value is already available to forward.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark spends a lot of time in tight load-fed loops. The current one-cycle
load-use bubble leaves the consumer idle even on zero-wait memory cycles
where the loaded value is already available to forward.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a ready-load bypass from MEM into EX so a dependent instruction can consume
the live load data on zero-wait cycles, while the classic load-use interlock
still fires whenever the memory response is not provably ready. That should
remove the common bubble without changing RVFI retirement order or the
stalled-bus behavior.

title: Pipeline the fetch translator
motivation:
CoreMark is still front-end sensitive, and the current IF loop computes
the next fetch PC directly from the live instruction word in the same
cycle it is returned. A tiny registered translate stage should break
that bus-to-PC feedback arc without changing retirement behavior.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark is still front-end sensitive, and the current IF loop computes
the next fetch PC directly from the live instruction word in the same
cycle it is returned. A tiny registered translate stage should break
that bus-to-PC feedback arc without changing retirement behavior.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-cycle fetch-translate stage in IF so the instruction word,
predicted next PC, and queue bookkeeping are first latched, then used on
the next cycle to advance `pc`. Keep the existing 2-entry buffer and
static backward/JAL policy, but make the address-generation decision
consume registered metadata instead of the live `io_imemData` path; this
should lift Fmax more than the extra cycle costs in IPC.

title: Retire-safe return stack
motivation:
CoreMark still spends time in helper-call and return-heavy paths. The
existing static backward/JAL predictor already covers the easy loop and
direct-call cases, so JALR returns are the remaining hot misses worth
attacking without widening the common branch path.

hypothesis.motivation:

existing static backward/JAL predictor already covers the easy loop and
direct-call cases, so JALR returns are the remaining hot misses worth
attacking without widening the common branch path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny IF-stage return-address stack that predicts only exact
`jalr x0, x1/x5, 0` returns when the top entry matches. Train it only
from architectural retirements: push return addresses for retired
JAL/JALR calls that write x1/x5, pop on retired returns, and saturate on
overflow instead of overwriting older entries. Keep the current static
backward/JAL heuristic as fallback and let EX redirect recover misses.

title: Exact source-use gating
motivation:
CoreMark mixes loads with many immediate and control-flow instructions, but the
current hazard and forwarding logic only sees raw rs1/rs2 bitfields. That creates
avoidable false dependencies and keeps the compare cone wider than it needs to be.

hypothesis.motivation:
CoreMark mixes loads with many immediate and control-flow instructions, but the
current hazard and forwarding logic only sees raw rs1/rs2 bitfields. That creates
avoidable false dependencies and keeps the compare cone wider than it needs to be.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Carry exact rs1/rs2-use bits with each fetched instruction, then use them to gate

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny IF-stage return-address stack that predicts only exact
`jalr x0, x1/x5, 0` returns when the top entry matches. Train it only
from architectural retirements: push return addresses for retired
JAL/JALR calls that write x1/x5, pop on retired returns, and saturate on
overflow instead of overwriting older entries. Keep the current static
backward/JAL heuristic as fallback and let EX redirect recover misses.
